summary: Joel Spolsky explains the five architectural principles behind CityDesk, Fog Creek's content management software. He argues for building a standalone Windows desktop application rather than following the prevailing web-based, XML-driven trends of the early 2000s. The principles are: web browsers can't deliver good UIs, XML is wrong for data storage, most users don't control their web servers, Windows is the only platform worth targeting with limited resources, and content management must be accessible to non-programmers. Spolsky frames these contrarian decisions as vindicated by user feedback praising CityDesk's ease of use, and credits Fog Creek's independence from VC groupthink for allowing them to make pragmatic rather than fashionable choices.
key_points: Web browser UIs impose latency, lack decent text editing widgets, and waste screen space on browser chrome, making them fundamentally inferior to native desktop apps for content creation.
XML is suitable for data interchange and settings files but is a poor choice for storing and querying large datasets compared to relational databases.
The vast majority of people maintaining websites have no ability to install server-side software, so a client-only architecture dramatically expands the addressable market.
Platform economics dictate Windows-first: every dollar invested in Windows yields 20x the sales of a Mac version, and Mac ports can cost more than the original due to missing equivalent libraries.
Making software 10% easier to use can double the potential user base, so CityDesk compromised on power and ubiquity rather than on simplicity.
money_quotes: Nobody wants to compose in a big TEXTAREA on an HTML page.
We're grateful that we're not in Silicon Valley where everyone meets at Bucks and Stanford University seminars and copies each other's bad ideas.
XML is a Dumb Format For Storing Data.
The next time some uninformed analyst at Gartner or Giga or Forrester tells you 'in the future, everything will be XML,' ask them how to do 'SELECT author FROM books' fast with XML.
The rule of thumb with ease-of-use is that if you make your program 10% easier, you'll double the potential number of users of your product.
Even I can't figure out XML and XSL, and I'm pretty sharp.
Thank you for keeping your dumb overfunded companies out of my way, because now, over here in GUI land, I can't find any real competition.
Despite romantic rhetoric, I really do need to pay the rent, so for now, you're going to have to rely on college kids and the occasional charitable big company for your Linux software.
key_insight: Building software based on pragmatic usability principles rather than industry hype yields better products and a competitive advantage when the hype cycle collapses.
